title: AI in Design Systems
description: Learn to integrate AI components into your design system — from documentation generation to pattern libraries to accessibility auditing with Claude.
learningObjectives: Design and document AI-specific components within a design system, including loading states, confidence indicators, and feedback mechanisms
Use Claude to generate, maintain, and audit design system documentation including usage guidelines, code examples, and accessibility notes
Apply AI-powered accessibility auditing techniques to identify and resolve compliance issues across design system components

sections.title: AI Components in Design Systems
sections.content:
As AI features become standard in digital products, design systems need to evolve to include components specifically designed for AI interactions. These components are fundamentally different from traditional UI components because they need to handle non-deterministic content, variable latency, and uncertainty — states that most design systems were never built to address.

**Why AI Components Need Their Own Category**

Existing design system components assume predictable behavior: a button click triggers a known action, a form field accepts defined input types, a card displays structured data. AI components break these assumptions in three important ways.

First, AI produces **non-deterministic output**. An AI-generated response component needs to handle text of unpredictable length, structure, and format. Unlike a product card that always has a title, image, and price, an AI response might be a single sentence or a multi-paragraph analysis with bullet points, code blocks, and tables. Your component must gracefully accommodate all of these possibilities without layout breakage.

Second, AI introduces **variable timing** into the interface. Processing takes anywhere from milliseconds to 30+ seconds depending on the task, which means components need built-in loading, streaming, and timeout states that traditional components simply don't require. A standard button resolves its action almost instantly, but an AI-powered action might leave the user waiting — and your component needs to communicate what's happening throughout that wait.

Third, AI outputs carry varying degrees of **confidence and uncertainty**. Components need ways to communicate confidence levels, source attribution, and verification status. These are concepts that don't exist in traditional UI, and they require entirely new visual patterns to convey effectively.

**Essential AI Component Types**

Every design system supporting AI features should include several foundational component categories:

• **AI response containers** display AI-generated content with appropriate labeling, formatting support, and action buttons such as copy, regenerate, edit, and expand/collapse. These containers should visually distinguish AI content from human content through subtle background colors, border treatments, or iconography, so users always know what was generated versus what was authored.
• **Streaming text components** display text as it's generated, character by character or word by word. These need cursor indicators, smooth animation, and the ability to transition from streaming to complete states without layout shifts. Getting this transition right is critical — a jarring reflow when streaming finishes undermines the sense of responsiveness that streaming is meant to create.
• **Confidence indicators** are visual elements that communicate how certain the AI is about its output. These range from simple labels like "High confidence" or "Needs review" to color-coded confidence bars to source citation badges. The right choice depends on how much your users need to evaluate AI reliability in context.
• **Feedback mechanisms** provide standardized ways for users to rate AI outputs: thumbs up/down buttons, satisfaction scales, "Report inaccuracy" links, and optional text feedback fields. These should be consistent across all AI features in your product so that users build a single mental model for providing feedback.
• **AI loading states** go beyond spinners. Consider skeleton screens shaped to the expected output, progress indicators with contextual labels like "Analyzing your document...", and cancelation affordances that let users bail out of long operations. These specialized loading patterns set appropriate expectations in a way that a generic spinner cannot.

**Designing for AI Component Variants**

Each AI component needs more variants than traditional components, and the difference is significant. Start with **state coverage** by mapping out every possible state: empty (no AI interaction yet), loading (AI is processing), streaming (content arriving in real time), complete (full response available), error (generation failed), timeout (took too long), and degraded (partial result available). Most traditional components have 3-4 states, but AI components often need 7 or more. Missing even one state creates an awkward experience when users inevitably encounter it.

**Content adaptability** is equally important. AI response containers must handle plain text, markdown, code, tables, lists, and embedded media. Test with the shortest and longest realistic outputs to ensure the component works at both extremes — a single-word response shouldn't look lost in an oversized container, and a lengthy analysis shouldn't overflow or become unreadable.

Don't forget **contextual variations**. The same AI feedback component might appear inline in a chat interface, in a sidebar panel, or in a modal. Design variants for each context with appropriate sizing and layout behavior, because a component that works perfectly in a wide content area may be completely unusable in a narrow sidebar.

sections.title: Generating and Maintaining Documentation with Claude
sections.content:
Design system documentation is essential but notoriously underfunded. Teams build components but skip the documentation that makes them usable by other designers and developers. Claude can dramatically lower the cost of creating and maintaining high-quality documentation, turning what used to be a multi-day writing project into a focused afternoon of reviewing and refining AI-assisted drafts.

**Component Documentation Generation**

For each component in your design system, Claude can draft comprehensive documentation if you provide it with the component's specifications. The key is giving Claude enough context to generate genuinely useful output rather than generic boilerplate. Three types of documentation are especially worth generating:

• **Usage guidelines** — Describe the component's purpose and constraints to Claude, and ask it to generate a guide that covers when to use this component, when not to use it (and what to use instead), and common misuse patterns to avoid. For example, you might prompt: "This is a toast notification component. Generate usage guidelines covering when to use toasts vs. banners vs. inline alerts, maximum content length, and auto-dismiss timing." The specificity of your prompt directly determines the quality of the output.
• **Props and API documentation** — For developer-facing docs, provide Claude with the component's props or API interface and ask it to generate descriptions for each prop, including type, default value, and a usage example. Claude is particularly good at generating code examples that show props in context, which is exactly what developers need to understand how a prop actually behaves.
• **Do/don't examples** — Ask Claude to generate pairs of correct and incorrect usage: "For this dropdown component, generate 5 do/don't pairs covering label clarity, option count, default selection, keyboard accessibility, and error states." These concrete examples are the fastest way for designers to understand component usage, far more effective than abstract guidelines.

**Pattern Documentation**

Beyond individual components, design systems need documentation for how components work together to create coherent experiences.

**Composition patterns** describe how components combine to create common UI patterns. Ask Claude to document these compositions: "Document how the search input, filter chip, results list, and pagination components combine to create a search experience. Include the interaction flow, state management between components, and responsive behavior." This kind of documentation is especially valuable because it captures institutional knowledge that often lives only in senior designers' heads.

For **layout guidelines**, provide your spacing scale and grid specifications, then ask Claude to write guidelines with visual descriptions of correct and incorrect spacing in common layouts. Layout documentation is tedious to write by hand but straightforward for Claude to generate when given clear constraints.

**Content templates** round out pattern documentation. Ask Claude to generate templates for each component that requires text content. For instance, a modal dialog template might specify that titles should follow a verb-plus-noun format of five words maximum, body text should be limited to one paragraph explaining the action and its consequences, and button labels should use a specific action verb rather than a vague "OK." These templates prevent the inconsistency that creeps in when every designer writes content from scratch.

**Maintaining Documentation Over Time**

The hardest part of design system documentation isn't writing it — it's keeping it current. This is where Claude becomes an ongoing partner rather than a one-time writing tool. Three maintenance strategies make the biggest difference:

• **Change-driven updates** — When a component is updated, describe the changes to Claude along with the existing documentation. Ask it to update the docs to reflect the changes, flag any sections that might be affected, and highlight any new usage guidelines needed. This turns a documentation update from a chore into a quick review task.
• **Consistency audits** — Periodically feed all your component documentation to Claude and ask it to identify inconsistencies: different terminology for the same concepts, conflicting guidelines between components, or missing sections that exist in other component docs. These inconsistencies accumulate silently over time and confuse anyone trying to learn the system.
• **Gap identification** — Ask Claude to review your design system documentation and identify what's missing. For example: "Here's a list of our 40 components and which ones have complete documentation. For the 12 components without docs, prioritize them by how frequently they're used and how complex they are." This gives your team a clear roadmap for documentation work.

playground.description: Try using Claude to generate component documentation for a design system. Edit the component details or ask for different documentation types (usage guidelines, do/don't pairs, content templates) to see how specificity shapes the output.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a senior design systems documentation writer. You produce clear, concise component documentation that both designers and developers can use. Follow design system documentation best practices: lead with purpose, include concrete usage guidance, and always specify what NOT to do. Format your output in clean markdown with headings, tables where appropriate, and short code-style prop references.
playground.userMessage:
Document the following design system component:

Component: StatusBadge
Purpose: Displays a short status label with a colored dot indicator.
Variants: success (green), warning (amber), error (red), info (blue), neutral (gray)
Props: label (string), variant (enum), size (sm | md | lg, default md), withDot (boolean, default true)
Usage context: Appears in tables, cards, and list items to show item state.

Generate:
1. A brief overview (2-3 sentences)
2. Usage guidelines — when to use StatusBadge vs. inline text, vs. Toast, vs. Banner
3. Three Do/Don't pairs covering label clarity, variant selection, and dot indicator usage
4. A props reference table

sections.title: Accessibility Auditing with AI
sections.content:
Accessibility is a requirement, not a feature, but it's often the last thing teams think about. Claude can serve as an always-available accessibility reviewer that catches issues before they reach users, helping teams build accessible components from the start rather than remediating after the fact.

**Component-Level Accessibility Review**

Every design system component should meet WCAG 2.1 AA standards at minimum. Claude can help you audit each component across four dimensions that matter most:

• **Color contrast analysis** — Describe your component's color values (text, background, border, and interactive state colors) and ask Claude to evaluate contrast ratios against WCAG requirements. The minimum is 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text and UI components. When your current values fall short, Claude can suggest alternative colors that maintain your brand palette while meeting requirements, saving you the trial-and-error of manual adjustment.
• **Keyboard interaction audit** — Describe how your component works with mouse interactions and ask Claude to define the equivalent keyboard interactions. Every mouse action needs a keyboard equivalent: click maps to Enter or Space, hover states need focus equivalents, and drag-and-drop needs arrow key or alternative input methods. This is one of the most commonly overlooked areas of accessibility, and Claude can systematically identify gaps.
• **Screen reader compatibility** — Describe your component's visual design and ask Claude to specify the ARIA roles, labels, and live region behavior it needs. Consider a practical example: "This component shows a real-time updating status indicator — a colored dot (green, yellow, or red) with a text label. What ARIA attributes does it need so screen reader users get the same information as sighted users?" The visual design communicates meaning through color and shape, but screen reader users need that same meaning conveyed through semantic markup.
• **Focus management** — Complex components like modals, drawers, and dropdown menus require careful focus handling. Ask Claude to specify where focus goes when the component opens, what happens when the user tabs past the last focusable element, and where focus returns when the component closes. Getting focus management wrong creates a confusing experience where keyboard users lose their place on the page.

**Pattern-Level Accessibility Review**

Individual components can be accessible in isolation but create accessibility problems when combined on a page. This is where pattern-level review becomes essential.

**Page-level landmark structure** determines how screen reader users navigate your interface. Describe a page layout composed of your design system components and ask Claude to evaluate the landmark structure: does the page have a single main landmark? Are navigation regions labeled? Are complementary regions like sidebars properly identified? Without clear landmarks, screen reader users have no way to jump between major sections of the page.

**Heading hierarchy** is another area where composition creates problems. Provide a page layout with all its headings and ask Claude to evaluate whether headings are in logical order (h1, h2, h3 without skipping levels), whether every section has a heading, and whether heading levels are used semantically for structure rather than visually for size. It's common for design systems to define heading styles by size, which tempts designers to choose headings based on how they look rather than what they mean.

**Error handling accessibility** deserves special attention for form patterns. Describe your form error pattern and ask Claude to evaluate it for screen reader users. The key questions are whether errors are announced when they appear, whether users can navigate from an error message to the field it relates to, and whether the error state is communicated through more than just color. Relying on color alone to indicate errors excludes users with color vision deficiencies.

**Building Accessibility into the Design System Process**

The most effective approach is integrating accessibility checks into every stage of your workflow rather than treating accessibility as a final review gate. Three practices make this integration concrete:

• **Design review checklists** — Ask Claude to generate an accessibility checklist specific to your design system. These checklists should cover the most common WCAG violations for each component type and be written so that designers without deep accessibility expertise can use them confidently. A good checklist turns accessibility from an expert-only concern into something every team member can contribute to.
• **Automated + manual testing guidance** — For each component, have Claude specify which accessibility aspects can be tested automatically (contrast ratios, ARIA attributes, heading structure) and which require manual testing (keyboard usability, screen reader experience, cognitive load). This distinction helps teams allocate testing effort realistically — automated tools catch perhaps 30% of accessibility issues, and the rest require human judgment.
• **Accessibility documentation templates** — For each component's documentation page, include an accessibility section. Ask Claude to generate a template that covers supported keyboard interactions, ARIA attributes, focus behavior, and known limitations. When accessibility information lives right next to usage guidelines, developers are far more likely to implement it correctly.

playground.description: Use Claude to audit a design system component for accessibility. Try changing the component description or asking about different accessibility dimensions (keyboard, screen reader, color contrast) to explore how thorough an AI-assisted audit can be.
playground.systemPrompt: You are an accessibility specialist reviewing design system components for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. For each component you review, be specific and actionable: cite the exact WCAG success criteria that apply, describe the expected behavior for assistive technology users, and provide concrete remediation steps for any issues you identify. Use markdown formatting with clear headings.
playground.userMessage:
Audit this design system component for accessibility:

Component: DropdownMenu
Behavior: A button triggers a floating menu of selectable options. The menu appears below the button on click. Each menu item can have an icon, label, and optional keyboard shortcut hint. Items can be grouped with divider lines. Some items can be disabled (shown with reduced opacity at 40%).

Current implementation details:
- Menu opens on button click, no keyboard trigger implemented yet
- Menu items are <div> elements with onClick handlers
- Disabled items have `pointer-events: none` and `opacity: 0.4`
- No ARIA attributes are currently applied
- Focus does not move into the menu when it opens

Please audit this component and provide:
1. A list of WCAG violations with specific success criteria references
2. Required ARIA roles and attributes
3. Complete keyboard interaction specification
4. Screen reader announcement expectations
5. Focus management requirements

sections.title: Design Token Management and Consistency
sections.content:
Design tokens are the atomic values that define your design system — colors, spacing, typography, shadows, animation timing, and more. They're the bridge between design decisions and code implementation. Claude can help manage, audit, and evolve your design token system to maintain consistency across products and platforms.

**Token Architecture with AI Assistance**

A well-structured token system has layers of abstraction, and Claude can help you think through and implement this architecture at every level:

• **Primitive tokens (base layer)** are raw values with no semantic meaning: `color-blue-500: #3B82F6`, `spacing-4: 16px`, `font-size-lg: 18px`. These are the building blocks from which everything else is composed. Ask Claude to help audit your primitives for completeness: "Here are my color primitives. Are there gaps in the scale? Are the increments between values consistent?" Gaps in your primitive scale force designers to use off-system values, which is exactly the kind of drift a token system is meant to prevent.
• **Semantic tokens (meaning layer)** map primitives to purposes: `color-primary: color-blue-500`, `spacing-component-padding: spacing-4`, `font-size-heading: font-size-lg`. This is where most of the design thinking happens. Ask Claude to review your semantic token names for clarity and consistency: "Are these names clear enough that a new team member would know when to use `color-primary` vs. `color-accent` vs. `color-highlight`?" Ambiguous names lead to inconsistent usage, which defeats the purpose of having tokens at all.
• **Component tokens (specific layer)** map semantic tokens to specific component contexts: `button-background-color: color-primary`, `card-padding: spacing-component-padding`. Ask Claude to identify components that are using primitive tokens directly instead of semantic tokens — these are consistency risks, because when you update a semantic token, those components won't reflect the change.

**Token Auditing and Consistency**

Over time, token systems drift. New values get added without removing old ones, different teams use different tokens for the same purpose, and naming conventions evolve inconsistently. Regular auditing is essential to keep the system healthy, and Claude can assist with three key audit types:

• **Usage audit** — Provide Claude with your token list and ask it to identify tokens that appear redundant, tokens with inconsistent naming patterns, and tokens that seem to be missing from the system. A prompt like "Here are my 200 design tokens — identify any naming inconsistencies, potential duplicates, and gaps in coverage" can surface problems that are invisible when you look at tokens one at a time.
• **Cross-platform consistency** — If your design system supports multiple platforms, ask Claude to compare token values across web (px), iOS (pt), and Android (dp) and flag discrepancies. A spacing value that looks right on web might render differently on mobile if the unit conversion isn't precise.
• **Token deprecation planning** — When you need to remove or replace tokens, describe the migration to Claude: "I want to replace these 5 legacy color tokens with 3 new semantic tokens. Generate a migration guide that shows which old token maps to which new token, and flag any cases where the mapping isn't one-to-one." Clear migration guides prevent the breakage and confusion that token changes typically cause.

**Using Claude for Theme Management**

Design tokens enable theming — light mode, dark mode, high contrast mode, and brand variations — and Claude can accelerate the creation and validation of theme variants.

For **dark mode token generation**, provide your light mode tokens and ask Claude to generate dark mode equivalents. Claude can apply established dark mode principles: invert luminance relationships while maintaining perceived contrast, adjust saturation for dark backgrounds, and ensure text remains readable. The goal isn't a mechanical inversion of every value but a thoughtful adaptation that preserves the same visual hierarchy and brand recognition.

**High contrast mode** is more constrained than standard dark mode. Ask Claude to generate a high contrast token set that meets WCAG AAA standards, which require a 7:1 contrast ratio for text. This demands careful color selection, because many brand colors don't meet AAA ratios without significant adjustment.

After generating theme variants, use Claude for **theme consistency validation**. Ask it to compare themes and flag inconsistencies: "In the light theme, the primary button has higher contrast than the secondary button. In the dark theme, they're almost the same. Is this intentional?" These kinds of cross-theme discrepancies are easy to miss during creation but obvious to users who switch between modes.

**Evolving Tokens Over Time**

Design tokens aren't static. As your product evolves, your token system needs to evolve with it, and Claude can help manage that evolution responsibly.

Before changing a token value, use Claude for **impact analysis**. Ask: "If I change `spacing-4` from 16px to 12px, which components will be affected? Based on our component documentation, which components might break with this smaller spacing?" Understanding the blast radius of a change before making it prevents the cascading breakage that makes teams afraid to update their token systems.

For **version management**, ask Claude to help draft release notes for token updates that explain what changed, why it changed, and what designers and developers need to do in response. Clear communication is the difference between a smooth migration and a flood of confused bug reports.

sections.title: Create Design System Documentation with Claude
sections.content: Practice using Claude to generate comprehensive design system documentation for a component.

exercise.prompt:
You're documenting a design system component: an AI Response Card. This component displays AI-generated text content within a product interface (not a chat — think inline AI suggestions, generated summaries, or analysis results).

The component has these features:
- Displays AI-generated text with markdown rendering support
- Shows an "AI Generated" label with a small sparkle icon
- Has three action buttons: Copy, Regenerate, and Dismiss
- Supports three states: Loading (skeleton with shimmer), Streaming (text appearing progressively), and Complete (full content visible)
- Has an optional confidence indicator (High / Medium / Low) shown as a colored badge
- Collapses to a summary with "Show more" for content longer than 150 words
- Includes a subtle feedback mechanism (thumbs up/down) that appears on hover

Create the complete documentation for this component:

1. **Overview** — A 2-3 sentence description of the component's purpose and when to use it.
2. **Usage guidelines** — When to use this component vs. other options (inline text, modals, chat bubbles). Include 3 "Do" examples and 3 "Don't" examples.
3. **Props/Configuration table** — List all configurable properties with name, type, default value, and description. Include at least 8 props.
4. **States documentation** — Describe all component states (loading, streaming, complete, error, collapsed, expanded) with specific guidance on transitions between states.
5. **Accessibility specification** — ARIA roles, keyboard interactions, screen reader announcements, and focus management for this component.
6. **Content guidelines** — Rules for the AI-generated content displayed in this component: maximum length, formatting support, and how to handle different content types (text, lists, code, tables).

Write documentation that a developer could use to implement the component and a designer could use to apply it correctly.
hints: The overview should focus on what makes this component different from a generic card — it's specifically designed for AI-generated content and includes features like streaming, confidence, and feedback that a standard card doesn't need
For the Do/Don't examples, think about common misuse: using this component for human-written content, hiding the AI label, or showing AI responses without any feedback mechanism
The props table should include props for all the features mentioned: content, loading state, streaming state, confidence level, callbacks for actions, feedback handlers, and collapse behavior

The accessibility specification needs to address how the streaming state is communicated to screen readers — continuously announcing new text would be disruptive, but saying nothing means screen reader users don't know content is appearing
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A clear, concise overview that positions the component relative to alternatives and establishes when to use it. (2) Usage guidelines with 3 specific Do/Don't pairs that address realistic misuse scenarios — not generic guidelines that apply to any component. (3) A props table with at least 8 properties covering all mentioned features, with accurate types, sensible defaults, and clear descriptions. (4) Complete state documentation covering all 6+ states with specific transition descriptions — not just 'loading transitions to complete' but the visual behavior during the transition. (5) An accessibility specification that addresses the unique challenges of AI content: streaming announcements (using aria-live with appropriate politeness), confidence indicator semantics, and feedback mechanism keyboard access. (6) Content guidelines that address markdown rendering, length handling, and edge cases (what happens with very short or very long AI responses). Deduct points for generic documentation that doesn't address AI-specific concerns, for missing props, or for accessibility specifications that don't handle the streaming state.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Integrating AI into your design system requires treating AI interactions as first-class design system citizens, not bolting them on as afterthoughts.

**AI components are a new category.** Non-deterministic output, variable latency, and uncertainty are fundamentally different from traditional UI states. Because of this, design system components for AI features need more variants, more states, and more careful documentation than standard components. Skipping any of these dimensions leads to awkward, inconsistent experiences when users encounter edge cases — and with AI, edge cases are the norm.

**Documentation is a design system feature, not a chore.** Claude dramatically reduces the cost of generating and maintaining component documentation, pattern guidelines, and content templates. The investment in documentation pays off every time a new team member learns the system, every time a designer makes a usage decision, and every time a developer implements a component correctly on the first try instead of guessing.

**Accessibility is non-negotiable.** Use Claude to audit components for WCAG compliance, generate keyboard interaction specifications, and create accessibility testing checklists. Building accessibility into the design system at the component level prevents entire teams from shipping inaccessible features — it's far more effective than relying on individual designers and developers to remember accessibility on every project.

**Tokens are the source of truth.** Design tokens ensure consistency across components, platforms, and themes. Use Claude to audit token systems for redundancy and drift, generate theme variants like dark mode and high contrast, and plan token migrations when your design language evolves. A well-maintained token system makes global design changes safe and predictable rather than risky and error-prone.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Apply what you've learned about AI in design systems to a realistic scenario.

exercise.prompt:
Your team is adding an AI-powered "Smart Suggestions" feature to your product. When a user is editing a document, a floating panel appears beside the cursor with AI-generated suggestions for improving clarity, tone, and grammar. The panel updates in real time as the user types.

You need to add this component to your design system. Describe:

1. What states does this component need beyond the obvious "showing suggestions" state? List at least five states and explain why each is necessary.
2. What accessibility challenges does a floating, auto-updating suggestion panel create, and how would you address each one?
3. Which design tokens would you define specifically for this component, and why shouldn't you hardcode these values?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: A strong response should: (1) Identify at least 5 component states beyond 'showing suggestions' — such as loading/analyzing, empty (no suggestions), dismissed, error/timeout, streaming/updating, collapsed, and offline/degraded — with clear reasoning for why each state matters to the user experience. (2) Address accessibility challenges specific to auto-updating floating content: screen reader interruptions from live region updates, focus management when the panel appears and disappears, keyboard dismissal and navigation, ensuring the panel doesn't obscure content for magnification users, and reduced motion considerations for real-time updates. Citing specific WCAG criteria (like 4.1.3 Status Messages or 2.4.3 Focus Order) strengthens the response. (3) Identify component-level tokens that reference semantic tokens — such as suggestion-panel-background, suggestion-panel-shadow, suggestion-text-color, suggestion-highlight-color, suggestion-spacing — and explain that hardcoding prevents theme consistency, makes dark mode adaptation impossible, and blocks global design updates from propagating to this component.
